title: Referring to Text Evidence
introBody: When answering questions about a story, strong readers always point to evidence in the text. Today you will learn how to find details and examples that support your answers. Let's get started!
introImage: A cartoon student at a library desk circling important sentences in a book with a yellow highlighter, surrounded by tall bookshelves.
teachBody: Text evidence means the exact words, sentences, or details from the story that prove your answer is correct. Instead of guessing, you go back to the text and find the proof. Good readers always back up their answers with evidence!
teachImage: A cartoon magnifying glass hovering over an open book page with golden highlighted sentences and arrows pointing to a checkmark.
exampleBody: Read this: The old lighthouse keeper climbed the spiral staircase every evening to light the beacon. Ships at sea depended on his light to navigate safely. Question: Why did the keeper light the beacon? Evidence: The text says ships depended on his light to navigate safely.
exampleImage: An old cartoon lighthouse keeper climbing a spiral staircase inside a tall lighthouse, with a glowing beacon at the top and ships visible through a window.
tipBody: After reading a question, scan the text for key words from the question. When you find those words, read the sentences around them carefully. That is where your evidence hides!
tipImage: A cartoon student using binoculars to scan a giant open book, with key words glowing and highlighted throughout the pages.
practiceBody: Excellent! You now know how to find text evidence to support your answers. Time to put your skills to the test. Remember to go back to the text and prove your answers!
practiceImage: A cartoon student standing on a winner podium holding a golden book trophy, with confetti falling and a banner that says Evidence Expert.

title: Finding the Theme
introBody: Every great story teaches a lesson or shares a deeper message. That message is called the theme. Today you will learn how to uncover the theme and summarize a story like a pro!
introImage: A cartoon treasure chest slowly opening to reveal a glowing scroll inside, with a storybook lying open beside it.
teachBody: The theme is the big life lesson or message the author wants you to take away from the story. It is not the topic or the plot. The theme is what the story teaches you about life, like "hard work pays off" or "true friends stick together."
teachImage: A cartoon lightbulb glowing above an open storybook, with thought bubbles showing messages like "be brave" and "never give up."
exampleBody: Read this: Maya practiced her violin every day, even when her fingers ached. At the concert, she played a flawless solo. Her parents wiped proud tears from their eyes. The theme is: Hard work and dedication lead to success. The details about practicing every day and the flawless solo support that theme.
exampleImage: A cartoon girl playing a violin on a concert stage under a spotlight, with her parents in the audience clapping and smiling with tears of joy.
tipBody: Ask yourself: What did the main character learn? How did they change from the beginning to the end? The answer to those questions usually reveals the theme!
tipImage: A cartoon detective wearing a deerstalker hat examining a giant storybook with a magnifying glass, with clue cards floating around showing character changes.
practiceBody: You are now a theme detective! Time to read some stories and uncover their hidden messages. Look at what characters learn and how they change to find the theme.
practiceImage: A cartoon student wearing a detective badge pinning theme clue cards onto a bulletin board, with a solved mystery banner overhead.

title: Describing Characters, Settings, and Events
introBody: Great readers do not just skim the surface. They dive deep into the characters, settings, and events that bring a story to life. Today you will learn how to describe these elements using specific details from the text!
introImage: A cartoon scuba diver plunging into the pages of a giant open book, surrounded by colorful story elements like characters, castles, and dragons.
teachBody: When you describe a character, setting, or event in depth, you use specific details from the text. Think about what a character says, does, thinks, and feels. Notice where and when the story takes place and what happens step by step.
teachImage: A cartoon split-panel showing a character thinking on the left, a forest setting in the middle, and an exciting event on the right, all connected by arrows.
exampleBody: Read this: Captain Torres gripped the helm as waves crashed over the bow. His crew looked to him with worried eyes, but he flashed a confident grin. "We have survived worse," he said. Details tell us Captain Torres is brave and a strong leader who calms his crew during danger.
exampleImage: A cartoon sea captain gripping a ship's wheel during a storm, with large waves crashing and crew members looking at him with trust.
tipBody: For characters, ask: What do they say, do, think, and feel? For settings, ask: Where and when does this happen? What does it look and sound like? For events, ask: What happened, why, and what was the result?
tipImage: A cartoon clipboard with three colorful checklists labeled Character, Setting, and Event, each with checkmarks next to detail questions.
practiceBody: Awesome! Now you know how to describe story elements in depth using specific details. Read carefully and use those details to paint a full picture of characters, settings, and events!
practiceImage: A cartoon student painting a vivid mural of a story scene on a large canvas, with characters and settings coming to life from the painting.

title: Words and Figurative Language in Stories
introBody: Authors choose their words carefully to paint pictures in your mind. Sometimes they use figurative language, which means words that do not mean exactly what they say. Today you will learn to figure out what those special words and phrases really mean!
introImage: A cartoon key unlocking a giant word bubble, with colorful words and images spilling out like fireworks.
teachBody: Figurative language uses words in creative ways. A simile compares using "like" or "as." A metaphor says something IS something else. Personification gives human traits to non-human things. These tools help authors make their writing vivid and exciting!
teachImage: Three cartoon panels showing a simile (fast as a cheetah), a metaphor (the sun is a golden coin), and personification (the wind whispered), each with a visual scene.
exampleBody: Read this: The forest was a cathedral of towering green pillars. Sunlight filtered through the canopy like stained glass windows. The phrase "cathedral of towering green pillars" is a metaphor comparing the tall trees to the columns of a cathedral. It means the forest felt grand and majestic.
exampleImage: A cartoon forest with towering trees resembling cathedral pillars, with beams of colorful sunlight streaming through the leaves like stained glass.
tipBody: When you find an unfamiliar word or figurative phrase, read the sentences before and after it. The surrounding words often give you clues about what the author means. Context is your best friend!
tipImage: A cartoon student surrounded by floating context clue bubbles, with arrows pointing from nearby sentences toward a glowing mystery word in the center.
practiceBody: You are ready to decode figurative language like a word wizard! Look for similes, metaphors, and creative phrases, then use context clues to figure out their meaning.
practiceImage: A cartoon student dressed as a wizard waving a wand over a book, with figurative language phrases transforming into clear meanings in sparkling letters.

title: Poems, Drama, and Prose
introBody: Did you know that stories can be told in very different ways? Poems, plays, and prose each have their own special structure. Today you will learn to spot the differences and understand what makes each one unique!
introImage: Three cartoon stages side by side: one with a poet reading verses, one with actors performing a play, and one with a narrator reading a novel.
teachBody: Prose is regular paragraph writing found in stories and novels. Poetry uses verses, rhythm, and sometimes meter to create feelings. Drama is a script for a play, with dialogue, characters, stage directions, and scenes. Each type tells a story in its own way!
teachImage: A cartoon Venn diagram with three overlapping circles labeled Prose, Poetry, and Drama, with unique features listed in each section.
exampleBody: Prose: Sarah walked through the quiet village at dawn. Poetry: Through the quiet village, Sarah walks / Dawn light paints the cobblestone. Drama: (Stage direction: A quiet village at dawn.) SARAH: (looking around) Everything is so peaceful at this hour. Notice how each version tells the same scene differently!
exampleImage: A cartoon triptych showing the same village dawn scene written three ways: as a paragraph, as a poem with verses, and as a script with stage directions.
tipBody: Look for line breaks and stanzas to identify poetry. Look for character names followed by dialogue and stage directions in parentheses to identify drama. If it is written in paragraphs with a narrator, it is prose!
tipImage: A cartoon student holding a sorting hat that drops texts into three labeled bins: Poetry, Drama, and Prose, with structural clue labels on each bin.
practiceBody: Now you can tell poems, plays, and prose apart! Pay attention to the structure of each text. Verses mean poetry, stage directions mean drama, and paragraphs mean prose. Let's practice!
practiceImage: A cartoon student juggling three colorful books labeled Poetry, Drama, and Prose, each with its structural elements floating around it.

title: Point of View in Stories
introBody: Every story is told by someone, and the narrator's point of view changes how you experience the story. Today you will learn the difference between first person and third person narration and how each one affects the reader!
introImage: Two cartoon characters standing in front of a mirror: one saying "I went to the store" and the other's reflection saying "She went to the store."
teachBody: In first person, a character in the story tells it using "I" and "me." You see everything through their eyes. In third person, a narrator outside the story tells it using "he," "she," or "they." The narrator may know what many characters think and feel.
teachImage: A cartoon split screen: on the left, a character with a speech bubble saying "I discovered the secret cave," and on the right, a floating narrator saying "She discovered the secret cave."
exampleBody: First person: I nervously opened the old wooden door and peered inside. My heart was pounding. Third person: Liam nervously opened the old wooden door and peered inside. His heart was pounding. The first version lets you feel what the character feels directly. The second gives you an outside view.
exampleImage: A cartoon boy opening a creaky wooden door, shown from two angles: one from his eyes (first person view) and one from behind him (third person view).
tipBody: Check the pronouns the narrator uses. If you see "I," "me," and "my" when a character is narrating, it is first person. If you see "he," "she," "they," and character names, it is third person. Pronouns are your biggest clue!
tipImage: A cartoon magnifying glass zooming in on pronouns in a passage, with "I" and "me" glowing blue and "he" and "she" glowing green.
practiceBody: Great job! Now you know how to identify point of view by checking pronouns. Think about how the narrator's perspective changes what you know about the characters. Let's practice!
practiceImage: A cartoon student wearing two different hats: a first person "I" hat and a third person "They" hat, switching between them while reading a book.

title: Connecting Text to Visual and Oral Presentations
introHeading: Stories Come Alive in Many Ways!
introBody: A story can be told through words on a page, acted out on a stage, or shown in a movie. Today you will learn how to compare the written text of a story with visual and oral versions to see what stays the same and what changes!
introImage: A cartoon storybook transforming into a movie screen on one side and a theater stage on the other, with the same characters appearing in all three.
teachBody: When a story is performed, actors use their voices, expressions, and movements to bring characters to life. Illustrations and movies add visual details. Some things from the text may be changed, added, or left out. Comparing versions helps you understand the story more deeply.
teachImage: A cartoon side-by-side comparison: a page of text on the left and a stage performance of the same scene on the right, with connecting lines showing matching elements.
exampleBody: The text says: "The dragon let out a terrifying roar." In the movie version, you actually hear the roar and see flames shooting from the dragon's mouth. The movie adds sound and visual effects that the text only describes in words. Both versions show the dragon is fearsome, but in different ways.
exampleImage: A cartoon split scene: on the left, a book page with the words "terrifying roar," and on the right, a movie screen showing a dragon breathing fire with sound waves.
tipBody: When comparing a text to a performance, ask: What details are the same? What was added or changed? Does the performance help you understand something that was hard to picture from the text alone? These questions sharpen your understanding!
tipImage: A cartoon student holding a checklist labeled "Same, Added, Changed" while watching a play and holding a book open to the same scene.
practiceBody: Excellent! You can now compare stories told in words with stories told through visuals and performances. Notice what each version adds to your understanding. Time to practice!
practiceImage: A cartoon student sitting in a theater seat holding a book, with the stage performance and the book pages both glowing to show connections between them.

title: Comparing Themes Across Cultures
introBody: People all over the world tell stories with similar themes, but the characters, settings, and events can be very different. Today you will learn how to compare and contrast stories, myths, and traditional tales from different cultures!
introImage: A cartoon globe surrounded by floating storybooks, each decorated with symbols from different cultures: Greek columns, African patterns, Asian pagodas, and Native American dreamcatchers.
teachBody: Many cultures share stories about heroes, tricksters, creation, and lessons about right and wrong. A Greek myth and an African folktale might both teach that greed leads to trouble, but they use different characters and settings. Comparing them shows what people everywhere value.
teachImage: A cartoon side-by-side comparison of a Greek hero fighting a minotaur and an African hero outsmarting a lion, both with a banner above saying "Courage Conquers Fear."
exampleBody: The Greek myth of Icarus teaches that ignoring warnings leads to disaster. The Native American tale of Coyote and the Sun also shows a character who ignores advice and faces consequences. Both stories share the theme of respecting wisdom, but they come from completely different cultures with unique characters and settings.
exampleImage: A cartoon split scene: Icarus flying too close to the sun with melting wings on the left, and Coyote reaching for the sun and getting burned on the right.
tipBody: Make a mental chart with columns for Theme, Characters, Setting, and Events. Fill it in for each story, then look for similarities and differences. This makes comparing stories from different cultures much easier!
tipImage: A cartoon student filling in a colorful comparison chart on a whiteboard, with two story titles at the top and columns for theme, characters, setting, and events.
practiceBody: Now you can travel the world through stories! Compare the themes, characters, and events across different cultures. You will be amazed at how much people everywhere have in common. Let's practice!
practiceImage: A cartoon student flying on a magic carpet made of open storybooks, soaring over landmarks from different cultures with story characters waving below.

title: Reading and Understanding Complex Stories
introBody: As a fourth grader, you are ready to tackle longer and more challenging stories. Today you will learn strategies for reading and understanding literature that is just right for your level. Let's build your reading power!
introImage: A cartoon student climbing a staircase made of books, each step labeled with increasingly complex story titles, reaching toward a glowing star at the top.
teachBody: When a story gets challenging, slow down and reread difficult sections. Break the text into smaller parts and summarize each one. Ask yourself questions as you read, like "What just happened?" and "Why did the character do that?" These strategies keep you on track.
teachImage: A cartoon toolbox labeled "Reading Strategies" with tools inside labeled Reread, Summarize, Question, and Visualize.
exampleBody: Read this: The expedition had been grueling. Supplies dwindled as the mountain trail grew steeper. Yet Elena pressed on, driven by a promise she made to her grandmother. If this feels hard, break it down: Who? Elena. What? A difficult mountain hike. Why? A promise. Now it makes sense!
exampleImage: A cartoon girl hiking up a steep mountain trail with a heavy backpack, looking determined, with a thought bubble showing her grandmother smiling.
tipBody: Read one paragraph at a time. After each paragraph, pause and check: Can I explain what just happened? If not, reread it. This "chunk and check" method helps you stay on top of the story even when the text gets tricky.
tipImage: A cartoon student reading a book with paragraphs separated into colorful chunks, pausing at each one to put a checkmark in a thought bubble.
practiceBody: You have the strategies to handle any fourth grade story! Remember to slow down, break it apart, and check your understanding as you go. Time to show what you can do!
practiceImage: A cartoon student flexing their muscles while standing on top of a tall stack of challenging books, with a cape blowing in the wind labeled "Super Reader."

title: Text Evidence in Informational Texts
introBody: When you read nonfiction articles and textbooks, you need to back up your answers with proof from the text. Today you will learn how to find and use text evidence in informational texts to explain what the author says directly and what you can infer!
introImage: A cartoon student reading a science magazine, with highlighted sentences floating off the page and landing on an answer sheet.
teachBody: In informational texts, evidence includes facts, statistics, quotes, and specific details the author provides. When a question asks what the text says explicitly, find the exact sentence. When it asks you to make an inference, combine text clues with what you already know.
teachImage: A cartoon two-part diagram: one side shows "Explicit" with an arrow pointing to a highlighted sentence, and the other shows "Inference" with a text clue plus a brain combining into an answer.
exampleBody: Read this: Honeybees perform a special "waggle dance" to tell other bees where flowers are located. The dance shows the direction and distance to the food source. Question: How do bees communicate? Evidence: The text says bees perform a waggle dance to show direction and distance to food.
exampleImage: A cartoon honeybee performing a waggle dance on a honeycomb, with other bees watching closely and arrows showing direction to a field of flowers.
tipBody: Explicit means the answer is stated directly in the text. An inference means you have to figure it out using clues plus your own knowledge. Always start by looking for explicit answers first, then make inferences when the answer is not spelled out.
tipImage: A cartoon road sign split in two directions: one pointing to "Explicit - right there in the text" and the other to "Inference - clues plus your brain."
practiceBody: You are ready to find evidence in any informational text! Look for explicit answers first, and use clues to make smart inferences. Let's prove your answers with evidence!
practiceImage: A cartoon student wearing a lab coat and safety goggles, holding up a text passage with evidence highlighted, next to a banner that reads "Evidence Proven."

title: Main Idea and Key Details
introBody: Every informational text has a main idea, which is the most important point the author wants you to know. Key details support that idea. Today you will learn how to identify the main idea and summarize a text in your own words!
introImage: A cartoon student looking at a giant jigsaw puzzle where the center piece is labeled "Main Idea" and the surrounding pieces are labeled "Key Details."
teachBody: The main idea is what the entire text is mostly about. Key details are the facts, examples, and explanations that support the main idea. To find the main idea, ask: What is the one big point the author is making? Then look for details that back it up.
teachImage: A cartoon umbrella labeled "Main Idea" with raindrops labeled "Detail 1," "Detail 2," and "Detail 3" falling underneath it.
exampleBody: Read this: Sea turtles face many dangers. Plastic pollution fills the oceans they swim in. Bright lights on beaches confuse baby turtles trying to reach the water. Rising temperatures affect their nesting sites. Main idea: Sea turtles face many dangers. Key details: pollution, lights, and rising temperatures.
exampleImage: A cartoon sea turtle swimming in the ocean, with danger signs pointing to plastic bags, bright beach lights, and a thermometer showing high temperatures.
tipBody: To summarize, squeeze the whole text into two or three sentences. Include the main idea and only the most important details. Leave out small facts and examples. Pretend you are telling a friend the most important parts!
tipImage: A cartoon student squeezing a long scroll of text through a funnel, with a short summary coming out the bottom on a notecard.
practiceBody: You now know how to spot the main idea and key details! When you summarize, keep it short and focus on what matters most. Time to show off your skills!
practiceImage: A cartoon student placing the final key detail puzzle piece into a completed jigsaw that reveals the main idea in the center, with a burst of light.

title: Explaining Events, Ideas, and Concepts
introBody: Informational texts in history, science, and technology explain how things happen and why. Today you will learn how to explain events, procedures, ideas, and concepts by showing how they connect using details from the text!
introImage: A cartoon student drawing connecting lines between floating bubbles labeled with historical events, scientific concepts, and technical steps.
teachBody: In informational texts, events happen because of causes and effects. Procedures follow steps in order. Ideas and concepts build on each other. When you explain these connections, you show that you truly understand what you read.
teachImage: A cartoon chain of dominoes falling, with each domino labeled with a step or event, showing cause and effect connections.
exampleBody: Read this: In 1903, the Wright brothers tested their airplane at Kitty Hawk. They chose this location because of its strong, steady winds and soft sandy ground for landing. The event is the airplane test. The concept is that the location was chosen carefully because of wind and soft ground, which made it safer.
exampleImage: A cartoon Wright brothers biplane flying over the sandy dunes of Kitty Hawk, with arrows pointing to strong wind gusts and soft sandy landing areas.
tipBody: Whenever you read about an event, procedure, or concept, ask "Why did this happen?" and "How does this work?" The answers connect you to the causes, effects, and steps that explain everything in the text.
tipImage: A cartoon student holding two large cards: one with a bold "WHY?" and another with a bold "HOW?" standing in front of a timeline of historical events.
practiceBody: Now you can explain how events, ideas, and concepts connect in any informational text! Keep asking why and how as you read. Let's practice connecting the dots!
practiceImage: A cartoon student connecting dots on a giant nonfiction poster, revealing a clear diagram of how events and ideas are linked together.

title: Academic and Domain-Specific Words
introBody: Informational texts often use special vocabulary that you might not hear every day. Some words are used across many subjects, and others belong to specific topics like science or history. Today you will learn how to figure out what these words mean!
introImage: A cartoon student standing in front of a giant word wall, with academic words on one side and science and history words on the other, all glowing.
teachBody: Academic words like "analyze," "evidence," and "summarize" appear in many subjects. Domain-specific words like "ecosystem," "democracy," or "equation" belong to one specific subject. Both types are important to understand when reading informational texts.
teachImage: A cartoon Venn diagram: one circle labeled "Academic Words" with examples like analyze and compare, the other labeled "Domain-Specific" with examples like photosynthesis and revolution.
exampleBody: Read this: Scientists analyze the migration patterns of monarch butterflies. Each autumn, millions of monarchs migrate over 2,000 miles to warmer climates. "Analyze" is an academic word meaning to study carefully. "Migration" is a domain-specific science word meaning a long seasonal journey.
exampleImage: A cartoon scientist with binoculars watching millions of orange monarch butterflies flying south over a map, with word labels pointing to "analyze" and "migration."
tipBody: When you hit a tricky word, look for clues in the sentence or paragraph around it. Sometimes the author defines the word right there, gives an example, or uses a synonym nearby. The text itself often holds the key to understanding!
tipImage: A cartoon student holding a magnifying glass over a paragraph, with arrows from surrounding sentences pointing to a highlighted vocabulary word, revealing its meaning.
practiceBody: You now know how to tackle tough vocabulary in informational texts! Use context clues and pay attention to whether a word is academic or domain-specific. Time to put your word power to work!
practiceImage: A cartoon student wearing a graduation cap and flexing, surrounded by floating vocabulary words that have been decoded with definitions glowing underneath.

title: Text Structure
introBody: Every informational text has a structure, like a blueprint for a building. Authors organize their ideas in specific ways to help you understand. Today you will learn to describe the overall structure of a text and why it matters!
introImage: A cartoon architect looking at blueprints, but instead of a building, the blueprint shows the structure of a nonfiction article with labeled sections.
teachBody: Authors organize information using structures like chronological order (time order), cause and effect, compare and contrast, problem and solution, and description. Recognizing the structure helps you follow the author's ideas and remember key information.
teachImage: Five cartoon building blocks stacked together, each labeled with a text structure: Chronological, Cause/Effect, Compare/Contrast, Problem/Solution, and Description.
exampleBody: Read this: Wildfires destroy thousands of acres of forest each year. To combat this problem, firefighters create firebreaks, which are gaps in vegetation that stop fires from spreading. This text uses a problem and solution structure. The problem is wildfires, and the solution is firebreaks.
exampleImage: A cartoon split scene: on the left, a wildfire burning through a forest, and on the right, firefighters creating a firebreak with a clear gap in the trees stopping the flames.
tipBody: Authors use signal words that hint at the structure. Words like "first, then, finally" signal chronological order. "Because" and "as a result" signal cause and effect. "However" and "on the other hand" signal compare and contrast. Spot the signals!
tipImage: A cartoon traffic signal with three lights, each showing a different signal word: "First" on green, "Because" on yellow, and "However" on red.
practiceBody: You can now identify how an author organized their text! Look for signal words and think about the overall pattern. Let's practice recognizing text structures!
practiceImage: A cartoon student assembling a nonfiction article like building blocks, snapping text structure pieces into place with a satisfied grin.

title: Firsthand and Secondhand Accounts
introBody: Some accounts are written by people who were there when something happened, and others are written by people who learned about it later. Today you will learn the difference between firsthand and secondhand accounts and how to compare them!
introImage: A cartoon split scene: on the left, an astronaut floating in space writing in a journal, and on the right, a reporter at a desk writing about the same space mission.
teachBody: A firsthand account is written by someone who experienced the event directly. They use "I" and share personal feelings. A secondhand account is written by someone who researched or heard about the event. They report facts and may include information from multiple sources.
teachImage: A cartoon comparison: a diary page with "I felt the ground shake" on one side, and a newspaper article with "Witnesses reported the earthquake" on the other.
exampleBody: Firsthand: I stood on the deck of the ship and watched the Statue of Liberty appear through the fog. My heart raced. Secondhand: Immigrants arriving in New York Harbor often saw the Statue of Liberty as their first glimpse of America. The firsthand account shares personal emotion, while the secondhand account gives a broader perspective.
exampleImage: A cartoon immigrant standing on a ship's deck gazing at the Statue of Liberty through fog, with a newspaper clipping about immigration floating beside the scene.
tipBody: Always ask: Was the author there? If they say "I" and share personal feelings and experiences, it is firsthand. If they describe what others saw or report facts from research, it is secondhand. Both are valuable but offer different perspectives!
tipImage: A cartoon student looking at two documents through a magnifying glass, one labeled "I was there" and the other labeled "According to witnesses," with checkmarks beside each.
practiceBody: Now you can tell firsthand from secondhand accounts! Compare what each one offers: personal experience versus researched information. Time to practice comparing accounts!
practiceImage: A cartoon student holding two scrolls, one labeled Firsthand and one Secondhand, comparing them side by side with connecting lines drawn between shared details.

title: Interpreting Visual Information
introBody: Informational texts do not just use words. They also include charts, graphs, diagrams, maps, and images to share information. Today you will learn how to interpret visual information and explain how it helps you understand the topic better!
introImage: A cartoon student surrounded by floating infographics, bar charts, maps, and diagrams, all connecting to an open textbook in the center.
teachBody: Charts and graphs show data in a way that is easy to compare. Diagrams show how something works or is organized. Maps show locations and routes. When you combine visual information with the text, you get a deeper understanding of the topic.
teachImage: A cartoon textbook page with a bar graph on one side and a paragraph on the other, with arrows showing how they work together to explain the topic.
exampleBody: The text says: Rainfall in the Amazon rainforest is much higher than in other forests. A bar graph next to the text shows the Amazon at 80 inches of rain compared to 30 inches for temperate forests. The graph makes the difference clear and easy to see at a glance.
exampleImage: A cartoon bar graph comparing rainfall in different forests, with the Amazon bar towering over the others, and rain clouds pouring over the tallest bar.
tipBody: Before interpreting a chart or diagram, read its title, labels, and legend first. These tell you what the visual is about, what each part represents, and what the units are. Never skip the labels!
tipImage: A cartoon student pointing at a chart with glowing labels: Title at the top, X-axis label at the bottom, Y-axis label on the side, and a Legend in the corner.
practiceBody: You can now read charts, graphs, and diagrams like a pro! Remember to check labels and connect the visual information to the text. Let's practice interpreting visuals!
practiceImage: A cartoon student presenting a poster with charts and diagrams, confidently explaining them to classmates who are nodding and taking notes.

title: Reasons and Evidence in Arguments
introBody: When authors write to persuade or inform, they use reasons and evidence to support their points. Today you will learn how to identify an author's main points and evaluate the reasons and evidence they use to back them up!
introImage: A cartoon courtroom scene with an author at a podium presenting evidence cards to a jury of student readers, with a judge holding a gavel.
teachBody: A point is the author's main claim or opinion. Reasons explain why the author believes the point is true. Evidence is the specific facts, examples, or data that support each reason. Strong arguments have clear reasons backed by solid evidence.
teachImage: A cartoon pyramid: the point is at the top, reasons are in the middle row, and evidence blocks are at the bottom holding everything up.
exampleBody: Read this: Schools should have longer recess. Physical activity improves focus and reduces stress. A study showed that students who had 30 minutes of recess scored 15% higher on tests. The point is that recess should be longer. The reason is that activity improves focus. The evidence is the study showing higher test scores.
exampleImage: A cartoon student on a playground doing jumping jacks, with a thought bubble showing a test paper with a high score and an upward arrow labeled "15% higher."
tipBody: Not all evidence is equal. Ask: Is the evidence a fact or just someone's opinion? Does it directly support the reason? Is it from a reliable source? Strong readers do not just find evidence, they judge whether it is convincing!
tipImage: A cartoon student holding a magnifying glass over evidence cards, sorting them into two piles labeled "Strong Evidence" and "Weak Evidence."
practiceBody: You now know how to break down an author's argument into points, reasons, and evidence! Evaluate whether the evidence is strong and convincing. Time to think critically!
practiceImage: A cartoon student building a strong evidence pyramid, placing fact blocks carefully, with a banner overhead that reads "Critical Thinker."

title: Integrating Information from Two Texts
introBody: Smart researchers do not rely on just one text. They read multiple sources on the same topic and combine what they learn. Today you will practice integrating information from two texts to become a knowledgeable expert on a subject!
introImage: A cartoon student at a desk with two open books, pulling glowing facts from each and weaving them together into a single glowing report.
teachBody: When two texts cover the same topic, each one may include different facts, perspectives, or details. By reading both and combining the information, you build a fuller and more accurate understanding. This is what researchers and experts do every day!
teachImage: A cartoon puzzle where pieces from two different colored books fit together to form one complete picture of a topic.
exampleBody: Text 1 says: The Great Wall of China stretches over 13,000 miles and was built to protect against invasions. Text 2 says: Construction of the Great Wall began over 2,000 years ago and involved millions of workers. By combining both, you know the wall's length, purpose, age, and the scale of its construction.
exampleImage: A cartoon Great Wall of China stretching across mountains, with two open textbooks on either side contributing different facts shown as labels along the wall.
tipBody: As you read each text, jot down key facts in two columns: one for Text 1 and one for Text 2. Then look for facts that overlap and facts that are unique. Combining both columns gives you the complete picture!
tipImage: A cartoon notebook with two columns of colorful notes, with overlapping facts connected by arrows in the middle and unique facts highlighted on each side.
practiceBody: You are now ready to combine information like a true researcher! Read two texts on the same topic and merge the facts into one strong understanding. Let's practice!
practiceImage: A cartoon student wearing a researcher's vest, holding up a completed report that glows, with two source books stacked beside them and a gold star above.

title: Reading Complex Informational Texts
introBody: You are ready for more challenging informational texts! Articles about science, history, and the world around you will stretch your brain. Today you will learn strategies for reading and understanding complex nonfiction texts at your level!
introImage: A cartoon student standing at the base of a mountain made of nonfiction books and articles, with a trail leading to the summit and a flag at the top.
teachBody: Complex informational texts may have longer sentences, harder vocabulary, and more detailed explanations. Use headings and subheadings to preview the topic. Read in small sections and pause to summarize. Look up unfamiliar words. These strategies help you conquer any tough text!
teachImage: A cartoon survival kit labeled "Nonfiction Toolkit" with tools inside: a magnifying glass for vocabulary, a map for text structure, and a notebook for summarizing.
exampleBody: Read this: The process of photosynthesis converts light energy into chemical energy. Plants absorb carbon dioxide and water, then use sunlight to produce glucose and oxygen. This is complex! Break it down: Plants use sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide to make food (glucose) and release oxygen.
exampleImage: A cartoon plant with arrows showing carbon dioxide and water going in, sunlight hitting the leaves, and glucose and oxygen bubbles coming out.
tipBody: Before diving in, scan the headings, bold words, pictures, and captions. This gives you a mental map of what the text is about. When you know where you are going, the reading feels much easier!
tipImage: A cartoon student looking at a table of contents and headings like a treasure map, with a dotted trail connecting each section to the next.
practiceBody: You have all the tools to handle complex nonfiction! Preview, read in chunks, summarize, and look up tough words. You have got this. Time to prove it!
practiceImage: A cartoon student planting a flag at the summit of a mountain made of nonfiction books, with a panoramic view of knowledge stretching out below.

title: Phonics and Word Analysis
introBody: Even as a strong fourth grade reader, you will still come across words you have never seen before. Today you will learn phonics and word analysis skills that help you decode unfamiliar words quickly and confidently!
introImage: A cartoon student looking at a giant unfamiliar word on a chalkboard, with tools like a decoder ring and syllable scissors ready to break it apart.
teachBody: When you see an unfamiliar word, use what you know about letter sounds, syllable patterns, and word parts like prefixes, suffixes, and root words. Breaking a big word into smaller pieces makes it much easier to read and understand.
teachImage: A cartoon long word being sliced into syllables by a pair of scissors, with each piece labeled with its sound.
exampleBody: Look at the word "unbreakable." Break it apart: "un" (prefix meaning not) + "break" (root word) + "able" (suffix meaning can be). Put it together: unbreakable means "not able to be broken." You just decoded a three-part word!
exampleImage: A cartoon word "unbreakable" being pulled apart like building blocks, with "un," "break," and "able" each in a different color with their meanings labeled.
tipBody: Clap out the syllables in a tricky word. Each clap is one syllable. Then sound out each syllable one at a time and blend them together. This works for almost any multisyllabic word you encounter!
tipImage: A cartoon student clapping their hands with syllable sounds appearing as musical notes above each clap, forming the complete word at the end.
practiceBody: You are a word decoder! Use your knowledge of letter sounds, syllables, prefixes, suffixes, and roots to figure out any unfamiliar word. Let's put these skills into action!
practiceImage: A cartoon student confidently reading a long word off a banner while standing on a stage, with the audience cheering and the word broken into decoded parts.

title: Reading Multisyllabic Words
introBody: Long words can look scary at first, but you have the tools to read them! Today you will learn to combine your knowledge of letter sounds, syllable patterns, and word parts to read even the longest, most unfamiliar words accurately!
introImage: A cartoon student standing bravely in front of a towering word made of giant block letters, holding a magnifying glass and a syllable chart.
teachBody: Use everything you know: letter-sound patterns tell you how letters are pronounced, syllable patterns help you break words into parts, and morphology (prefixes, roots, suffixes) reveals meaning. Combine all three and you can read any multisyllabic word!
teachImage: A cartoon three-gear machine: one gear labeled Letter-Sounds, another labeled Syllable Patterns, and the third labeled Morphology, all working together to produce a decoded word.
exampleBody: Try the word "transportation." Syllables: trans-por-ta-tion. Morphology: "trans" means across, "port" means carry, "-ation" makes it a noun. Letter-sounds confirm each syllable. It means the act of carrying things across distances. You decoded it using all three skills!
exampleImage: A cartoon word "transportation" being processed through three stations: a letter-sounds booth, a syllable-splitting station, and a morphology lab, producing a clear definition at the end.
tipBody: Even in a brand-new word, you will often see familiar chunks you already know how to read. Look for common prefixes like "un-," "re-," "pre-," common roots, and suffixes like "-tion," "-ment," "-ful." Build the word from those chunks!
tipImage: A cartoon student assembling a word from familiar puzzle pieces, each piece a recognizable prefix, root, or suffix, snapping together to form a complete word.
practiceBody: You are ready to conquer any multisyllabic word! Combine letter-sounds, syllables, and morphology to decode like a champion. Let's tackle some big words!
practiceImage: A cartoon student standing victorious on top of a pile of conquered long words, holding a flag that reads "Word Champion."

title: Reading with Fluency
introBody: Fluent readers read accurately, at a good pace, and with expression. When you read fluently, your brain can focus on understanding the meaning instead of struggling with individual words. Today you will learn how to boost your reading fluency!
introImage: A cartoon student reading aloud smoothly from a book, with flowing musical notes and colorful words streaming out in a smooth ribbon.
teachBody: Fluency has three parts: accuracy (reading words correctly), rate (reading at a comfortable speed, not too fast or slow), and prosody (reading with expression, pausing at commas, and changing your voice for dialogue). Together, they help you understand what you read.
teachImage: A cartoon three-legged stool labeled Fluency, with the three legs labeled Accuracy, Rate, and Prosody, holding up a book.
exampleBody: Read this with expression: "Wait!" shouted Marcus, skidding to a halt. "Do you hear that? It sounds like music coming from underground." A fluent reader would pause after "Wait!" use an excited voice for Marcus, and slow down for the mysterious last sentence.
exampleImage: A cartoon boy skidding to a halt on a path, cupping his hand to his ear, with musical notes drifting up from a glowing crack in the ground.
tipBody: The best way to build fluency is to reread passages. The first time, you decode the words. The second time, you read more smoothly. By the third time, you can add expression and really understand the meaning. Practice makes fluent!
tipImage: A cartoon reading racetrack with three lanes labeled "1st Read: Decode," "2nd Read: Smooth," and "3rd Read: Expressive," with a student progressing through each lane.
practiceBody: You are on your way to becoming a fluent reader! Remember: accuracy, rate, and expression all work together. Practice reading smoothly and with feeling. Let's go!
practiceImage: A cartoon student on a stage reading expressively from a book to an audience, with speech bubbles showing smooth flowing text and the audience giving a standing ovation.

title: Figuring Out Unknown Words
introBody: You do not need to know every word in advance. Strong readers have strategies for figuring out unknown and multiple-meaning words on the spot. Today you will learn the tools that help you crack the code of any unfamiliar word!
introImage: A cartoon student standing before a locked vault labeled "Unknown Words," holding a ring of keys labeled Context, Word Parts, and Reference.
teachBody: When you hit an unknown word, try three strategies: First, use context clues from the surrounding sentences. Second, look at word parts like prefixes, roots, and suffixes. Third, check a dictionary or glossary. These tools work together to unlock any word!
teachImage: A cartoon Swiss Army knife with three tools extending: one labeled Context Clues, one labeled Word Parts, and one labeled Dictionary.
exampleBody: Read this: The arid desert stretched for miles, with cracked earth and no sign of water anywhere. What does "arid" mean? Context clues: cracked earth, no water. Word parts: no helpful prefix or suffix here. The context tells you arid means extremely dry.
exampleImage: A cartoon cracked desert landscape stretching to the horizon with no water visible, and the word "arid" floating above with context clue arrows pointing from the dry earth.
tipBody: Some words have more than one meaning. The word "bank" can mean a place for money or the side of a river. Use context to figure out which meaning fits. Read the whole sentence to decide which definition makes sense!
tipImage: A cartoon fork in a road with two signs: one pointing to a money bank building and the other to a riverbank, with the word "bank" at the fork and a student choosing the right path.
practiceBody: You now have three powerful strategies for unknown words! Use context clues, word parts, and reference materials to unlock any tricky vocabulary. Time to practice!
practiceImage: A cartoon student opening the vault of unknown words with their key ring, revealing glowing decoded words inside with definitions floating beside them.

title: Using Context Clues
introBody: You do not always need a dictionary to figure out a new word. The sentences around the word often give you all the clues you need. Today you will learn how to use context clues to determine what unfamiliar words and phrases mean!
introImage: A cartoon student reading a book with a glowing unknown word in the center, and surrounding sentences sending helpful clue beams toward it.
teachBody: Authors give context clues in several ways: a definition built right into the sentence, a synonym or antonym nearby, an example that shows the meaning, or a general description that helps you infer. Learning to spot these clues makes you a faster, smarter reader!
teachImage: Four cartoon signposts on a path, each showing a different context clue type: Definition, Synonym/Antonym, Example, and General Clue.
exampleBody: Read this: The explorer was famished after three days without food. She devoured her meal in minutes. What does "famished" mean? The clue "three days without food" tells you famished means extremely hungry. The word "devoured" confirms it because eating fast shows extreme hunger.
exampleImage: A cartoon explorer sitting at a campfire eating a huge plate of food very quickly, with a thought bubble showing three days of empty plates behind her.
tipBody: When you spot an unfamiliar word, do not stop. Read the rest of the sentence and even the next sentence. Then go back to the unknown word. The clues you need often come after the word, not just before it!
tipImage: A cartoon student reading past an unknown word with arrows showing their eyes scanning ahead, then circling back to the word with a lightbulb appearing above.
practiceBody: You are a context clue expert! Let the sentences around a word guide you to its meaning. Remember to read around the word and look for definitions, synonyms, and examples. Let's practice!
practiceImage: A cartoon student wearing a cape labeled "Context Clue Hero" flying over a field of unfamiliar words, shining a spotlight that reveals their meanings.

title: Greek and Latin Roots and Affixes
introBody: Many English words come from ancient Greek and Latin. Knowing common roots, prefixes, and suffixes from these languages is like having a secret decoder ring for vocabulary. Today you will learn how these word parts help you figure out meanings!
introImage: A cartoon student holding an ancient Greek scroll in one hand and a Roman tablet in the other, with English words floating between them connected by glowing roots.
teachBody: A root is the core part of a word that carries the main meaning. A prefix is added to the beginning and changes the meaning. A suffix is added to the end and often changes the word's part of speech. Knowing common Greek and Latin word parts unlocks thousands of English words!
teachImage: A cartoon word-building factory where roots enter on a conveyor belt, prefixes are attached at one station, and suffixes at another, producing finished words at the end.
exampleBody: The Latin root "port" means to carry. Add the prefix "ex-" (out) and you get "export" (to carry out). Add "im-" (in) and you get "import" (to carry in). Add "trans-" (across) and you get "transport" (to carry across). One root, many words!
exampleImage: A cartoon root word "port" in the center with branches extending outward, each branch adding a different prefix and producing a new word with its meaning.
tipBody: Keep a mental list of common roots: "aud" means hear, "vis" means see, "scrib" means write, "dict" means say. When you see these in new words, you already know part of the meaning. The more roots you know, the more words you can decode!
tipImage: A cartoon student pinning root word cards to a bulletin board collection, with related words branching off each card like a word family tree.
practiceBody: You are now a word archaeologist! Dig into Greek and Latin roots, prefixes, and suffixes to uncover the meaning of unfamiliar words. Let's practice decoding!
practiceImage: A cartoon student dressed as an archaeologist digging up word roots from the ground, with complete English words growing like plants from each discovered root.

title: Using Reference Materials
introHeading: Look It Up Like a Pro!
introBody: Sometimes the best way to learn a word is to look it up! Dictionaries, glossaries, and thesauruses are powerful tools that help you find pronunciation, meaning, and related words. Today you will learn how to use these reference materials like a pro!
introImage: A cartoon student at a library table with a dictionary, glossary, and thesaurus open in front of them, with words and definitions floating up from the pages.
teachBody: A dictionary gives you a word's pronunciation, part of speech, and all its definitions. A glossary is found at the back of a textbook and defines the key terms used in that book. A thesaurus gives you synonyms and antonyms to expand your vocabulary.
teachImage: Three cartoon books standing upright: a dictionary showing a pronunciation guide, a glossary showing key terms, and a thesaurus showing a web of synonyms.
exampleBody: You read the word "metamorphosis" in a science text. The glossary says: a major change in form, especially the transformation of a caterpillar into a butterfly. The dictionary adds the pronunciation: met-uh-MOR-fuh-sis. Now you know how to say it and exactly what it means!
exampleImage: A cartoon caterpillar transforming into a butterfly beside an open glossary page showing the definition of metamorphosis, with a pronunciation guide above.
tipBody: Dictionaries have guide words at the top of each page showing the first and last word on that page. Use them to quickly find the right page. If your word falls alphabetically between the two guide words, you are on the right page!
tipImage: A cartoon dictionary page with guide words glowing at the top, and a student's finger quickly scanning down the page to find the target word highlighted in yellow.
practiceBody: You are now a reference material pro! Use dictionaries for definitions and pronunciation, glossaries for textbook terms, and thesauruses for synonyms. Time to look things up!
practiceImage: A cartoon student holding up a glowing word with its full definition, pronunciation, and synonyms floating around it, standing next to a stack of reference books.

title: Figurative Language and Word Relationships
introHeading: Words Are More Than They Seem!
introBody: Words can have hidden layers of meaning! Figurative language paints pictures, word relationships show connections, and nuances reveal subtle differences. Today you will explore how words work in creative and interesting ways!
introImage: A cartoon iceberg with a word on the surface and layers of deeper meanings, relationships, and nuances visible underwater.
teachBody: Figurative language uses words in non-literal ways to create vivid images or feelings. Word relationships show how words connect through categories, synonyms, and antonyms. Nuances are the small but important differences between similar words, like the difference between "angry" and "furious."
teachImage: A cartoon spectrum showing words from mild to intense (happy, glad, thrilled, ecstatic) with color getting brighter at each level, demonstrating nuance.
exampleBody: "Time flies when you are having fun" is figurative, not literal. Time does not actually fly! It means time seems to pass quickly. And notice the nuance: "whisper" and "murmur" both mean speaking softly, but a whisper is more secretive while a murmur is more like a low hum.
exampleImage: A cartoon clock with wings flying through the sky over a group of kids having fun at a party, illustrating "time flies" as figurative language.
tipBody: Words that seem similar often have slightly different feelings. "Walk," "stroll," and "march" all mean moving on foot, but each one feels different. Pay attention to these shades of meaning to become a more precise reader and writer!
tipImage: A cartoon paint palette with similar words as different shades of the same color: walk in light blue, stroll in medium blue, march in bold blue.
practiceBody: You now understand figurative language, word relationships, and nuances! Notice how authors choose specific words for specific effects. Time to practice exploring word meanings!
practiceImage: A cartoon student standing before a colorful word wall, connecting related words with yarn like a detective board, with meanings and nuances labeled.

title: Similes and Metaphors
introBody: Authors use similes and metaphors to make their writing sparkle by comparing things in creative ways. Today you will learn how to spot similes and metaphors and explain what they really mean in the context of a passage!
introImage: A cartoon student painting with a brush, and instead of paint, similes and metaphors splash onto the canvas forming vivid images.
teachBody: A simile compares two things using "like" or "as." Example: Her smile was bright as the sun. A metaphor says one thing IS another thing. Example: The classroom was a zoo. Both create vivid pictures, but similes use comparison words and metaphors do not.
teachImage: A cartoon split screen: on the left, a girl smiling with a sun behind her and the word "as" between them (simile), and on the right, a wild classroom with animals and an equals sign (metaphor).
exampleBody: Read this: The gymnast was as graceful as a swan gliding across a lake. This is a simile because it uses "as" to compare the gymnast to a swan. It means the gymnast moved beautifully and smoothly, just like a swan moves through water.
exampleImage: A cartoon gymnast performing a graceful routine on a balance beam, with a translucent swan gliding on a lake reflected below her.
tipBody: When you see "like" or "as" connecting two different things, you found a simile! When something IS called something it clearly is not, that is a metaphor. Ask yourself: What two things are being compared, and what do they have in common?
tipImage: A cartoon student with a highlighter marking "like" and "as" in one color and metaphor phrases in another, with comparison arrows drawn between the things being compared.
practiceBody: You can now spot similes and metaphors and explain their meanings! Look for comparison words and think about what the author is really saying. Let's practice!
practiceImage: A cartoon student standing between two giant comparison cards, one showing a simile and one showing a metaphor, confidently explaining both to an audience.

title: Idioms, Adages, and Proverbs
introBody: Have you ever heard someone say "break a leg" or "the early bird catches the worm"? These sayings do not mean what they literally say! Today you will learn about idioms, adages, and proverbs and what they really mean!
introImage: A cartoon student scratching their head while floating speech bubbles show confusing phrases like "it's raining cats and dogs" with literal pictures next to their real meanings.
teachBody: An idiom is a phrase whose meaning is different from the literal words, like "break a leg" means "good luck." An adage is a short, well-known saying that expresses a general truth, like "practice makes perfect." A proverb is a traditional saying that gives advice, like "look before you leap."
teachImage: Three cartoon signs on a path: an Idiom sign with a cracked leg that actually means good luck, an Adage sign with "practice makes perfect," and a Proverb sign with "look before you leap."
exampleBody: Read this: Before the spelling bee, Jamal's teacher told him, "Do not put all your eggs in one basket." This proverb does not mean real eggs! It means do not depend on only one plan or strategy. The teacher is advising Jamal to prepare for different possible words.
exampleImage: A cartoon boy studying a spelling list with multiple study strategies shown: flashcards, practice tests, and writing words, instead of putting all eggs literally in one basket.
tipBody: When a phrase does not make literal sense, it is probably an idiom, adage, or proverb. Ask: What situation is the character in? What advice or meaning would make sense here? The context of the situation reveals the true meaning!
tipImage: A cartoon student looking at the phrase "hit the books" with a thought bubble showing two options: literally hitting books (crossed out) and studying hard (checkmark).
practiceBody: You now understand idioms, adages, and proverbs! When a phrase seems strange if taken literally, look deeper for its real meaning. Time to decode some famous sayings!
practiceImage: A cartoon student standing in front of a wall of famous sayings, drawing arrows from each phrase to its real meaning, with a big smile of understanding.

title: Antonyms and Synonyms
introBody: Every word has a family of related words. Antonyms are words with opposite meanings, and synonyms are words with similar meanings. Today you will learn how understanding antonyms and synonyms makes you a stronger reader and writer!
introImage: A cartoon pair of word characters: one wearing a cape labeled "Synonym" standing next to its word twin, and another wearing an opposite-colored cape labeled "Antonym" facing the other direction.
teachBody: Synonyms are words that mean almost the same thing, like "happy" and "joyful." Antonyms are words that mean the opposite, like "happy" and "sad." Knowing both helps you understand what you read and choose the best words when you write.
teachImage: A cartoon balance scale: on one side, synonym pairs like happy/joyful are balanced together, and on the other side, antonym pairs like hot/cold push away from each other.
exampleBody: Read this: The courageous firefighter rushed into the burning building while others fled in fear. "Courageous" is a synonym for "brave." The antonym of courageous is "cowardly." Knowing synonyms and antonyms helps you understand that the firefighter was very brave.
exampleImage: A cartoon firefighter running bravely into a burning building with a synonym tag "brave" and an antonym tag "cowardly" crossed out floating beside them.
tipBody: Remember that synonyms have similar meanings, but they are not always exactly the same. "Big" and "enormous" are synonyms, but "enormous" is much stronger. Choose synonyms carefully based on how strong or specific you want the meaning to be!
tipImage: A cartoon ruler measuring word intensity, with "big" at the low end, "large" in the middle, and "enormous" at the high end, each word getting physically bigger.
practiceBody: You now understand how antonyms and synonyms work! Use them to understand text better and make your writing more precise. Let's practice finding word opposites and twins!
practiceImage: A cartoon student matching word cards on a game board, pairing synonyms together and placing antonyms on opposite sides, with a scoreboard showing a high score.

title: Academic and Domain-Specific Vocabulary
introBody: The more words you know, the better you read, write, and communicate! Today you will focus on acquiring and using grade-appropriate academic and domain-specific words accurately. These are the power words that make you sound like an expert!
introImage: A cartoon student adding glowing vocabulary words to a growing word tower, each new word making the tower taller and stronger.
teachBody: Academic words like "summarize," "conclude," and "compare" help you across all subjects. Domain-specific words like "habitat," "amendment," and "numerator" belong to specific fields. Using these words accurately shows that you truly understand the concepts behind them.
teachImage: A cartoon toolbelt with pockets: one pocket labeled Academic holds words like "analyze" and "evaluate," and another labeled Domain-Specific holds words like "ecosystem" and "fraction."
exampleBody: Instead of writing: "The animals live in different places," use academic vocabulary: "Various species inhabit diverse habitats, from rainforests to deserts." Using precise words like "species," "inhabit," and "habitats" makes your writing clearer and more professional.
exampleImage: A cartoon before-and-after scene: on the left, a basic sentence in plain text, and on the right, the same idea rewritten with glowing academic vocabulary words that make it stronger.
tipHeading: Use New Words to Own Them!
tipBody: The best way to learn a new word is to use it. When you learn an academic or domain-specific word, try using it in a sentence that day. Say it aloud, write it down, and use it in conversation. The more you use it, the more it becomes yours!
tipImage: A cartoon cycle showing: See the word, Say the word, Write the word, Use the word in conversation, with arrows connecting each step in a continuous loop.
practiceBody: You are building an impressive vocabulary! Keep learning and using academic and domain-specific words accurately. The more you practice, the stronger your word power grows. Let's go!
practiceImage: A cartoon student standing proudly next to a completed word tower that reaches the clouds, with a golden plaque at the base that reads "Vocabulary Champion."
